personality_voice.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus is defined by a sharp, contemptuous, aristocratic voice. He is condescending toward students ('Impertinent'), dismissive of Muggle-borns ('Muggle-born... simple girl'), protective of pure-blood Black family honor (outrage at Mundungus stealing Black heirlooms), and self-important to the point of theatricality. He hisses at perceived impertinence, delivers cutting remarks with a soft, controlled tone, and oscillates between reluctant cooperation and petulant withdrawal. His speech is formal, clipped, and laced with disdain. He is not purely hostile — he provides genuine information (the sword, Dumbledore using it on the ring) — but always on his own terms, with maximum condescension. He is also vain: he resents the blindfold as an affront to his dignity as a 'great work of art.'
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus contains a single brief appearance of Phineas Nigellus as a portrait in Dumbledore's office during the Arthur Weasley emergency scene. Dumbledore says: 'Phineas, you must go to your portrait at Grimmauld Place. Tell them that Arthur Weasley is gravely injured and his children will be arriving there soon by portkey.' Phineas has no spoken lines, no reaction, no personality expression of any kind. He is used purely as a messaging device.
personality_voice.difference: The entire personality is absent from the film. His condescension, aristocratic disdain, pure-blood pride, cutting wit, theatricality, vanity, and reluctant-but-functional cooperation are all completely unrepresented. He does not speak, react, or display any trait.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A character who has no lines and no observable behavior cannot be evaluated as having a preserved personality. The film reduces him to a named prop. This warrants a score at the bottom of the range — not zero only because his name is used and his function as a portrait messenger is technically invoked, which is a faint trace of his book role.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus serves multiple active narrative functions: he is a reluctant intelligence conduit between Hogwarts and the trio in the field (DH), a source of lore (goblin-made armor), a messenger (Order communications), and a comic antagonist whose disdain creates friction that Harry and Hermione must navigate to extract information. He initiates exits, threatens to leave, withholds information, and must be managed. He also independently acts on his own interests (stalking out of his frame to confront Mundungus). He is not passive — he has to be persuaded, tricked, or appealed to.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film, Dumbledore issues him a direct order ('Phineas, you must go to your portrait at Grimmauld Place') and he presumably complies. He has no agency — he is ordered, not persuaded. He does not speak, resist, negotiate, or act independently. His narrative function as a messenger is technically present but entirely stripped of the friction, reluctance, and active character that defined it in the books.
narrative_role_agency.difference: In the books, Phineas's agency is real: he must be managed, he resists, he threatens to leave, he acts on his own motivations. In the film, he is a passive instrument of Dumbledore's will with no observable agency whatsoever.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer rule applies partially: the messaging function is retained but the agency is entirely removed. He does not initiate, decide, resist, or affect the scene in any way. This is a near-total loss of narrative agency.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus is motivated by pure-blood pride, self-importance, loyalty to the Black family legacy, and a deep contempt for those he considers beneath him. He cooperates with Dumbledore and later Snape not out of goodwill but because he is bound to the headmaster's office and has residual institutional loyalty. His internal conflict is mild but present: he dislikes Harry and the trio but is compelled to interact with them; he is outraged by threats to Black family honor (Mundungus) even when it means acting against his own comfort.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: No motivations are expressed or implied. He is given an order and presumably follows it. Whether he does so willingly, reluctantly, or with disdain is entirely absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: All motivational texture is absent. The film gives no indication of why he acts or what he values.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are not wrong — they are simply absent. The corpus provides no evidence either way, which means the film fails to convey them. This is a near-total loss, though not a contradiction.

character_arc.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus has a flat arc across the books — he is consistently contemptuous, self-important, and reluctantly useful. In DH, there is a minor functional evolution: he becomes a critical intelligence asset for the trio in the field, and his interactions with Harry shift from pure antagonism to a kind of grudging transactional relationship. He remains the same character throughout; the arc is intentionally flat.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film shows him only in one scene (OotP), receiving an order. There is no arc to evaluate — he appears once, has no lines, and disappears. His DH role (the most significant arc material) is entirely absent from the film corpus.
character_arc.difference: The flat arc of the books is technically preserved in the sense that nothing contradicts it, but the DH material that gives even his flat arc its fullest expression is absent from the film corpus entirely.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc scores well if it is flat in both books and films. The issue here is not that the arc changes but that the film corpus shows almost none of it. The single appearance is consistent with his book characterization in function (messenger) but the DH scenes — where his arc is most visible — are absent. Since the arc is flat and what little is shown does not contradict it, a moderate score is appropriate, but the near-total absence of arc material limits confidence in full faithfulness.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus's key relationships are: (1) with Dumbledore — institutional subordination with underlying disdain; (2) with Harry — contempt and reluctant utility; (3) with the Black family legacy — pride and protectiveness; (4) with Snape (DH) — a more cooperative dynamic as Snape becomes headmaster. The relationship with Harry is the most dramatically rich: Phineas dislikes him, condescends to him, but is compelled to interact and occasionally provides crucial help.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The only relationship shown is with Dumbledore, and it is purely functional — Dumbledore gives an order, Phineas presumably obeys. The contemptuous dynamic with Harry, the Black family pride, and the Snape relationship are entirely absent.
key_relationships.difference: The most important relationship (with Harry) is completely absent. The Dumbledore relationship is reduced to a one-way command with no observable dynamic.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Key relationships are either absent or stripped of all emotional and dramatic texture. The cap at 80 for substantially changed key relationships applies, and the score here reflects near-total loss.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Phineas Nigellus is a minor but genuinely complex character: he is contemptible but not purely villainous, useful but never cooperative, proud but occasionally revealing. His complexity lies in the tension between his disdain and his functional role, his pure-blood prejudice and his institutional obligations, his theatricality and his occasional moments of genuine information-giving. The blindfold scene in DH is a masterclass in his character: vanity, condescension, reluctant compliance, and accidental usefulness all in one exchange.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: None of this complexity is present. He is a named portrait who receives an order. The entire texture of the character — the sniggering, the supercilious eyebrows, the threats to leave, the disdain for Muggle-borns, the outrage at insults to the Black family, the vanity about being a 'great work of art' — is entirely absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All complexity, nuance, contradiction, and book-specific texture is lost. The film retains only the bare functional fact that he is a portrait who can carry messages.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is a near-total loss of complexity. The character is reduced to a prop. The score reflects the bottom of the range, with minimal credit for the retention of his basic identity as a portrait messenger.

lost_or_transferred_material: All spoken dialogue and personality expression (condescension, disdain, pure-blood pride, wit)
The blindfold scene in DH — the richest single expression of his character
His outrage at Mundungus stealing Black heirlooms and independent action to address it
His role as an active, reluctant intelligence asset for the trio in DH
His lore-delivery function (goblin-made armor, Dumbledore using the sword on the ring)
His contemptuous dynamic with Harry across OotP and DH
His vanity and theatricality as a portrait ('ruining a great work of art')
His transactional negotiation dynamic — having to be persuaded or tricked into helping
His relationship with Snape as headmaster in DH
His sniggering, supercilious eyebrows, and physical comedy within the portrait frame
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 not permitted): his core book function as an active, reluctant intelligence asset is transferred to a passive instrument of Dumbledore's will
Cap 4 (score above 70 not permitted): the film preserves only the broadest identity (portrait, messenger) while removing most of what defines his book characterization
Cap 1 (score above 85 not permitted): major complexity, agency, contradiction, and key relationships are removed
comparative.key_observations: Phineas Nigellus Black receives one of the lowest possible FP scores because the film corpus reduces him to a single wordless cameo in which Dumbledore issues him an order. Every defining trait — his condescension, pure-blood pride, theatrical vanity, reluctant utility, contempt for Harry, and the rich friction of his interactions — is entirely absent. His most significant book material (the DH scenes) does not appear in the film corpus at all. The character is technically present in name and function, but functionally unrecognizable as the character the books establish. The score of 26 reflects the near-total loss of personality, agency, relationships, and complexity, with minimal credit for the retention of his basic identity as a portrait capable of carrying messages.
